desc: Quito is a strong base for exploring Ecuador with distinct seasonal trade-offs between weather comfort, crowd levels, and pricing.
hls: Seasonal climate profile benchmarked for Quito
Balanced mix of culture, food, and day-trip potential
Works for both short breaks and longer stays
Monthly data supports weather-first trip timing

todo.name: Walk central Quito
todo.desc: Use a half-day orientation walk through the historic/core districts and local markets.

todo.name: Museum and heritage stop
todo.desc: Anchor one cultural visit to understand local history and identity.

todo.name: Local food circuit
todo.desc: Plan a focused evening around regional specialties and neighborhood dining.

todo.name: Scenic viewpoint session
todo.desc: Schedule sunrise or sunset from a top city/nearby viewpoint.

todo.name: Nature or coast day trip
todo.desc: Reserve one full day for nearby nature, coast, or mountain access.

todo.name: Flexible slow day
todo.desc: Keep one low-structure day for weather pivots and spontaneous plans.

wifi.note: Urban hotels/cafés are usually stable; speeds can soften in outer districts or remote day-trip zones.

prac.fltNote: Nonstop UK service to Quito is limited/unreliable; most itineraries connect via Madrid, Amsterdam, or US hubs.

airports.name: Mariscal Sucre International Airport
transferOptions: Official airport taxis and app rides are door-to-door; late-night reliability is usually best with pre-booked pickup.
Aeroservicios airport bus to Quito (Terminal Quitumbe northbound connection) plus onward taxi/Metro for final leg.
Private transfer via hotel/tour operator is the most predictable option for first-night arrivals with heavy luggage.
airports.timingBands: ~45-60 min in light traffic, ~60-90 min in typical daytime traffic, and 90+ min in peak/rain disruptions.
arrivalTimeConstraints: Early morning (05:30-08:30): city-bound congestion builds quickly; budget extra buffer for same-day intercity buses or tours.
Midday/afternoon (12:00-19:00): rain and incidents on Ruta Viva/Simón Bolívar can create long stop-go sections.
Late evening/night (20:00-05:30): faster road times are common, but fewer bus frequencies and stricter check-in cutoffs for some hotels/apartments.

airports.name: José Joaquín de Olmedo International Airport
transferOptions: Use domestic connection GYE→UIO when fares/times align to avoid long overland transfer after arrival in Ecuador.
Intercity coach or private car from Guayaquil to Quito for budget routing, then local taxi/Metro in Quito.
airports.timingBands: Overland transfer is typically 7-10+ hours depending on route, mountain weather, and stops.
arrivalTimeConstraints: Same-day road transfer after a long-haul flight can be fatiguing; many travelers overnight in Guayaquil first.
Night mountain driving may face reduced services and weather-related slowdowns, so daytime departures are usually safer/logistically simpler.

prac.currency: Check live exchange rates before booking.
prac.lang: Local language plus variable English in tourist zones.
prac.visa: Always verify entry rules based on passport and transit routing before travel.
alerts: Historic Center access can tighten with short-notice demonstrations or security cordons; confirm road status before committing timed museum or dinner bookings in Centro Histórico.
At ~2,850 m elevation, Quito can cause headache/fatigue on arrival day—avoid hard hikes (e.g., Rucu Pichincha/Cotopaxi approaches) until you have at least one sleep cycle in the city.
Afternoon storms can interrupt views and cable operations on high-viewpoint plans (including TelefériQo); keep summit/outdoor visits in the morning and retain a weather backup activity.

first48h: First 6-12 hours: hydrate steadily, eat light meals, and keep activity to short flat walks around your hotel area.
Hours 12-24: avoid alcohol-heavy nights and intense cardio; prioritize sleep and monitor for persistent headache, nausea, or unusual breathlessness.
Hours 24-48: progress to moderate city sightseeing, but postpone major elevation gains until symptoms are clearly settled.
routePlanning: Schedule Quito as the first stop in Ecuador before higher points (e.g., Cotopaxi trails, Quilotoa rim, Papallacta passes) to improve acclimatization odds.
If landing late, keep day 1 local and place long day-trips on day 2 or 3 to reduce cancellation risk from fatigue/weather.
Build flexible starts for mountain excursions because overnight recovery varies; early symptom checks reduce unsafe push-through decisions.

sweet: Typically, target the best weather-comfort shoulder months to balance conditions, crowds, and pricing.
